summary: Lexington, Kentucky, is where the Kentucky Theatre is located and where a lot of the theater's history unfolded. In the early 1990s, after the theater had been closed for about four and a half years, the city was negotiating with potential operators but eventually local Lexington residents stepped up to reopen it. During this time, Lexington also saw the arrival of two Cinemark theaters, which made it challenging for the Kentucky Theatre to regain its audience. The city owned several theaters downtown, including the Kentucky and the State Theatre, and renovations and reopenings happened through the 1990s, shaping Lexington's cinema scene.

Howard Stovall was one of the two local Lexington individuals who stepped up to save the Kentucky Theatre after it had been closed for years. Alongside Anna Lee Scorsone, Howard took on the challenge of reopening and managing the theater despite having no prior experience in the theater business. He had operated several successful businesses before, which helped in managing the theater's reopening and operations. Their efforts were crucial in reestablishing the Kentucky Theatre in the early 1990s.

Anna Lee Scorsone was the other key local figure who helped save the Kentucky Theatre in Lexington after it had been closed for about four and a half years. She had a background in administration with Bluegrass Community and Technical College and the Kentucky Community and Technical College System, which gave her valuable experience for managing the theater. Together with Howard Stovall, she took on the responsibility of reopening and operating the theater in the early 1990s, playing a vital role in its revival.
cues: Kentucky Community and Technical College System

summary: Bluegrass Community and Technical College, or BCTC, is where Anna Lee Scorsone worked for a number of years in administration before helping to manage the Kentucky Theatre. Her experience there was part of what equipped her to step into the theater business despite not having prior direct experience. BCTC is part of the local educational landscape in Lexington and contributed indirectly to the theater's management through Anna Lee's background.

entity: Kentucky Community and Technical College System
summary: The Kentucky Community and Technical College System, or KCTCS, was part of Anna Lee Scorsone's professional background before she helped manage the Kentucky Theatre. She worked in administration there for several years, which gave her valuable skills that she later applied to running the theater. This connection highlights how local educational institutions played a role in supporting community cultural efforts like the theater's reopening in Lexington.

Cinemark Theatres entered the Lexington market in January 1990, opening two eight-screen complexes: one at Lexington Green and another called Man o' War Cinemas. Their arrival during the Kentucky Theatre's closure created competition that made it harder for the Kentucky to regain its audience. Cinemark's presence marked a shift in Lexington's cinema landscape in the 1990s, with newer multiplexes attracting moviegoers.

Man o' War Cinemas was one of the two Cinemark multiplexes that opened in Lexington in 1990, located on the east end of town on North Locust Hill Drive. I remember attending its opening night and knowing the manager from previous theater work. This theater was part of Cinemark's strategy to establish a strong foothold in Lexington, which posed a challenge to the Kentucky Theatre's efforts to reopen and attract audiences.

New York City comes up in my story because the manager of Man o' War Cinemas had worked there for a concession company before returning to manage Cinemark theaters in Alabama and then Lexington. His experience in New York City was part of his career path in the theater business, which I was familiar with from recommending him for a previous position. It shows how theater professionals often move between cities and roles in the industry.

Greyhound Lines played a surprising role in the logistics of running the Kentucky Theatre back in the 1990s. We often had to pick up film cans shipped by Greyhound, which was a significant part of managing repertory films. It was a hands-on experience, sometimes feeling like I spent half my time going to Greyhound to collect the film prints needed for screenings.

Louisville, Kentucky, figures into the story because some of the film prints we used at the Kentucky Theatre would be shipped onward to theaters there, like the Vogue Theatre. The repertory film prints circulated between cities including Lexington and Louisville, which was part of the challenge in maintaining quality prints for screenings. Louisville's theaters were part of the regional cinema network we interacted with during the 1990s.

The Vogue Theatre in Louisville was one of the venues that received film prints after they were shown at the Kentucky Theatre in Lexington. This sharing of prints was common among repertory theaters in the region during the 1990s. Unfortunately, the Vogue eventually closed, reflecting the challenges faced by independent and repertory cinemas at the time.

Larry Thomas was our film booker who also operated a theater called The Movies in Cincinnati. He played a key role in advising us to shift from purely repertory programming to more selective first-run films to adapt to changing cinema trends. His experience and insights were invaluable as we navigated the challenges of running the Kentucky and State Theatres in the late 1990s.

The Movies in Cincinnati was a theater operated by Larry Thomas, our film booker. It closed before we made programming changes at the Kentucky Theatre, signaling shifts in the repertory and independent cinema scene. The closure of The Movies was part of a broader trend affecting similar theaters in the region during the late 1990s.

Cincinnati, Ohio, is where Larry Thomas operated The Movies theater and where the Tri-State Theatre Booking Service is based. We worked closely with these Cincinnati-based entities for film booking and programming at the Kentucky Theatre. Cincinnati's theater scene and services influenced how we managed film bookings and adapted to industry changes in the 1990s.

The Belcourt Theatre, located in Nashville, was another repertory cinema that closed around the same time as The Movies in Cincinnati. Its closure was part of a regional pattern of repertory theaters struggling in the late 1990s. This trend influenced how we at the Kentucky Theatre reconsidered our programming strategies.

The State Theatre in Lexington was part of the city’s theater holdings purchased along with the Kentucky Theatre. While the Kentucky was renovated and reopened in the early 1990s, the State Theatre remained closed until 1996 when the city decided to renovate and reopen it. After reopening, the State Theatre often showed more commercial films while the Kentucky focused on more arty and repertory programming, complementing each other downtown.

The Kentucky Theatre is the heart of this story. After being closed for about four and a half years until 1992, it was saved and reopened thanks to local efforts by Howard Stovall and Anna Lee Scorsone. We focused on repertory and revival films, including foreign and classic titles, and adapted over time to include more selective first-run films. The theater has long been a cultural fixture in Lexington, navigating competition, renovations, and changing film distribution through the 1990s and beyond.

Tri-State Theatre Booking Service, based in Cincinnati, has been our trusted film booking partner for decades. Founded by Phil Borak, who has since passed away, the company is now run by two individuals who continue to represent the Kentucky Theatre with film studios like Paramount and Warner Bros. They handle negotiations and ensure we get the films we want under the terms set by the studios, making them an essential part of our operations.

Phil Borak was the founder of the Tri-State Theatre Booking Service, the company that has represented the Kentucky Theatre for many years. He was instrumental in building the relationships and trust with major film studios that allow us to book films reliably. Although he has since passed away, his legacy continues through the current owners who maintain those important connections for us.

Paramount Pictures is one of the major film studios we work with through our booking service, Tri-State Theatre Booking Service. They set the terms for how their films are shown, including release dates, the length of runs, and sometimes require upfront payments. Our relationship with Paramount is built on trust and adherence to their conditions, which is typical for first-run films at the Kentucky Theatre.

Warner Bros. is another major studio whose films we book at the Kentucky Theatre through Tri-State Theatre Booking Service. Like Paramount, they provide strict guidelines for film showings, including opening dates and run lengths. Our booking service has built a reputation with Warner Bros. that allows us to secure quality films for our audiences while following their rules.

The Girl Scouts of the USA have been a partner in a long-running program at the Kentucky Theatre for over thirty years. We host special screenings where the theater provides the space and the Girl Scouts pay for the film, offering free admission to attendees who bring donations like warm clothing or food. This partnership supports local charitable efforts and community engagement through cinema.

The Nest is a local organization in Lexington that benefits from donations collected during our joint programs with the Girl Scouts at the Kentucky Theatre. Attendees bring items like warm clothing, food, or hygiene products, which are then donated to The Nest to support women and children in need. This connection highlights how the theater serves not just as entertainment but also as a community resource.

A Christmas Story is one of the films we've shown at the Kentucky Theatre as part of special programming, often tied to community events like those with the Girl Scouts. It’s a beloved holiday classic that draws audiences and supports our mission to provide quality and meaningful film experiences. Films like this help us connect with families and the broader community during the holiday season.

Disney films are part of the broader range of movies we consider for programming at the Kentucky Theatre, especially when looking at quality first-run films. While we focus on more arty and repertory films, Disney's offerings sometimes fit into our schedule, particularly for family-friendly events. Our film bookers coordinate with studios like Disney through Tri-State Theatre Booking Service to select appropriate titles.

The Rocky Horror Picture Show is one of the cult classics that fits well with the repertory and revival programming at the Kentucky Theatre. Films like this have a dedicated audience and contribute to the theater's identity as a place for offbeat and classic cinema. Showing Rocky Horror helps us maintain a diverse and engaging calendar for our patrons.

Wolf Wiles is not mentioned directly in my story, so I don't have a personal connection or experience with them related to the Kentucky Theatre or the cinema operations I've described. My focus has been on local Lexington theaters, film booking services, and community partnerships rather than this organization.
